analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Jack Della Maddalena -- Style & Profile

- Jack Della Maddalena arrives in Perth as a hometown hero with an 18-3 professional record, but UFC stats are entirely absent — everything here is derived from his pre-UFC career, which means the read carries real uncertainty about how his skills translate at this level of competition.
- His pre-UFC record tells the story of a finisher: the majority of his wins have come by stoppage, and he built his reputation in Australian promotions before earning his UFC contract. The quality of that opposition is the central question mark — regional finishes don't automatically translate to elite welterweight.
- What footage and reputation suggest is an aggressive, forward-pressure striker who likes to work at mid-range and close distance. He's a boxer-first fighter who pressures opponents to the fence and looks for the finish rather than points.
- The reach disadvantage here is significant and structural — Prates has five inches on him, which means Della Maddalena will have to eat jabs and straight punches getting inside every single time he tries to engage. That's not unsolvable, but it's a real problem against a power striker.
- Perth is his home city. The crowd support will be real, but home-country advantages don't move win probability enough to flip a matchup — it's worth a small edge on composure under pressure, nothing more.
- Without any UFC-level striking or grappling averages, the honest read is that this is a debut performance with significant unknowns. How does he react to elite power? Does his chin hold? Does his wrestling exist? Those questions are genuinely unanswered.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Carlos Prates -- Style & Profile

- Prates is one of the most dangerous knockout artists in the welterweight division right now. Seven of his last eight wins have come by KO/TKO, and two of those — back-to-back — were against Leon Edwards and Geoff Neal, fighters who have combined for almost no KO losses in their careers. That's not regional-level finishing; that's elite-level finishing.
- He's a switch-stance karateka whose power is real and whose timing is exceptional. The stance switching creates constant alignment problems for opponents, and his 78-inch reach lets him land before most welterweights can get inside to answer. He generates knockout power without being a plodder — he's quick, not just big.
- His striking numbers tell an interesting story about his game plan. Against Geoff Neal, he threw at 6.42 strikes per minute. Against Leon Edwards — a far more dangerous counter-striker — he slowed to 1.39 per minute and still got the finish in round two. That adaptability is a sign of fight IQ, not just athleticism.
- The one real data point against him is the Ian Machado Garry fight, where he threw only 0.28 strikes per minute over five rounds in a decision loss. Garry neutralized his forward pressure with footwork and range management. A fight that goes to the scorecards is not Prates's friend — he needs to find the finish.
- His strike defense is 47% — below average for a ranked welterweight — and he absorbs 4.53 significant strikes per minute, which is a high number. He gets hit. He's willing to trade and eat shots to land his own. Against a fighter with real power, that's a calculated risk that has paid off recently, but it's a vulnerability that the right opponent could exploit.
- At 32 with 167 days since his last fight, he's fresh, healthy, and at the peak of his career. No layoff concerns, no obvious age cliff. He's fighting with momentum and intent after back-to-back wins over former and near-title-level competition.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The structural story of this fight is straightforward: Prates has a massive reach advantage, documented knockout power against elite opposition, and a five-fight finishing streak that includes names most welterweights have never beaten. Della Maddalena has an 18-3 record built largely in Australian regional competition, with zero UFC fights and no UFC-level data to assess. The line opened with Prates as a small favorite and has since swung heavily in his direction — the market has moved from a near-coin-flip to Prates being clearly favored, and that move reflects exactly what the data shows. Prates is the significantly more proven fighter at this level.

The key exchanges will happen at distance. Prates's 78-inch reach means he can jab, throw switch kicks, and land straight punches before Della Maddalena is in range to fire back. For JDM to win, he needs to close that gap without getting clipped on the way in — a tall order against someone who just knocked out Leon Edwards, a man with one of the best chins in the history of this division. Prates's willingness to absorb shots in exchanges does give Della Maddalena a theoretical path: if JDM can time the clinch or force scrambles in the pocket, he might find openings. But the Garry fight showed that Prates can also be neutralized by disciplined movement — and if JDM tries to use footwork rather than pressure, he's unlikely to out-point a fighter with Prates's size and timing over five rounds.

The net intangible picture heavily favors Prates. Della Maddalena is making his UFC debut against a fighter who just knocked out the former champion. The debut adjustment is real — cage size, glove differences, and the quality jump all apply — and the opponent here is not the soft landing that many debuting fighters receive. Prates has fought a 5-round main event, has momentum, and is not carrying any known physical or preparation concerns. The only genuine uncertainty is whether Della Maddalena carries a tool that his pre-UFC record didn't reveal — a grappling game, iron chin, or elite movement — but there's no data to support betting on an unknown positive.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 — Carlos Prates wins by KO/TKO, Rounds 1–3**
Likelihood: High
Why: Prates has finished seven of his last eight opponents, including a former champion and a durable veteran in his two most recent fights. Della Maddalena's chin is completely untested at this level, and Prates's reach advantage means JDM will absorb clean shots getting inside every time he commits. The most likely version of this fight ends on the feet before the championship rounds.

**#2 — Carlos Prates wins by KO/TKO, Rounds 4–5**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Della Maddalena has a better chin than expected and can survive the early exchanges, this fight could extend into the later rounds — but Prates has shown he can pace himself over five rounds, and a fighter absorbing that much damage early rarely improves late. A late finish remains well within Prates's range even if JDM makes it interesting early.

**#3 — Carlos Prates wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Prates doesn't win decisions often and doesn't particularly try to — his path to victory is the finish. If Della Maddalena survives the full 25 minutes and gives Prates the kind of movement problems that Garry did, a decision win for Prates is possible, though the judges in Perth with a hometown crowd makes scoring less predictable. Treat this as the backup if the stoppages don't land.

**#4 — Jack Della Maddalena wins by KO/TKO**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Prates absorbs 4.53 significant strikes per minute and has shown he'll take shots to land his own — which means JDM, if he has real power and can time the exchanges, does have a puncher's path. The caveat is that Prates has never been stopped in his UFC run, and his durability against top opposition is better than his defense numbers suggest. Possible, but requires JDM to be a significantly better fighter than his pre-UFC record proves.

Confidence: 67%

Prates opened as a coin-flip opponent for Della Maddalena and has since moved to a clear favorite, and that movement tracks with what the data actually says. The best available line on Prates is -110 at BetOnline.ag and several other books.

**Line math:**
- Best line on Prates: -110 (BetOnline.ag)
- Implied probability at -110: 52.4%
- Best line on Della Maddalena: -102 (FanDuel)
- Implied probability at -102: 50.5%
- Combined implied: 102.9% — de-vigged true probability on Prates: approximately 50.9%
- Assessed probability for Prates: 67%
- Edge vs. de-vigged market: approximately 16 percentage points

The edge here is large enough to warrant a second look for errors — and the honest answer is that the massive gap is almost entirely explained by one factor: this is a UFC debut with zero UFC data against a proven elite finisher. The market opened this fight as nearly a coin flip, likely because Della Maddalena has a regional following and the home-country angle inflated his line. The current -110 on Prates is still generous given the information asymmetry.

Quarter Kelly stake recommendation: **2.0 units** on Prates -110 (BetOnline.ag, DraftKings, or BetUS).
Target line: Prates up to -145 still carries value. Above that, reassess.

**Data context:** Prates has full UFC fight history. Della Maddalena has zero UFC fights — this is a debut with no UFC-level stats available. Any confidence above a moderate level on JDM would require inventing data that doesn't exist. The uncertainty cuts both ways in theory, but the opponent quality gap is documented on Prates's side.

**Market read:** The line has moved sharply toward Prates — from +100 at open to -175 at BestFightOdds current, with Della Maddalena going from +150 to around -110 at most books. No reverse line movement is present. The movement is directional and consistent toward Prates, which aligns with the stat-based read. The best available number on Prates (-110) is meaningfully better than where the line is heading — this may not last.

**Hedge verdict: MARGINAL ARB**
Best Della Maddalena line: -102 (FanDuel) → implied 50.5%
Best Prates line: -110 (BetOnline.ag) → implied 52.4%
Combined implied: 102.9% — no true arb. Take the value pick on Prates at -110. Do not hedge.

**Props worth considering:** A "fight ends before the final bell" prop or "Prates wins by KO/TKO" prop deserves attention if available at reasonable juice. Prates has finished 7 of 8 recent opponents, Della Maddalena's chin is untested at this level, and five rounds provides ample time for Prates to find the finish. The decision scenario exists but is the low-probability outcome here. Check the de-vigged number on any KO/TKO prop before playing — books price Prates finishes aggressively given his recent run.

**Conditions that would change this read:** If credible reporting emerged that Della Maddalena has legitimate high-level grappling that was suppressed in his regional career, or if his UFC debut came against lesser opposition (it does not — Prates is the second-ranked contender), or if Prates came in with a known injury, this read shifts. As presented, the bet is on what the data shows: a documented elite finisher against a debuting regional fighter with no UFC track record.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Beneil Dariush -- Style & Profile

- Dariush built his reputation as one of the more complete lightweights in the UFC — a wrestler with sharp hands, good submission awareness, and the kind of patient pressure that wore down Tony Ferguson and Mateusz Gamrot. That version of him, however, is increasingly difficult to find in recent fight footage.
- He has been knocked out in the first round in three of his last four fights — by Charles Oliveira (R1, 4:10), Arman Tsarukyan (R1, 1:04), and most recently Benoit Saint Denis (R1, 0:16). That 16-second knockout is the clearest signal of all: he is being stopped earlier each time, by opponents with varying levels of KO power.
- His striking output has collapsed. Career average is 3.78 significant strikes per minute, but his last three fights show 11.25 (a brawl he got stopped in), 1.47, and 1.88 — the two wins/survivals came from volume suppression and survival mode, not output. His accuracy also dropped to 25% against Tsarukyan, suggesting his timing is deteriorating.
- The grappling that made him dangerous has completely disappeared from recent footage. He attempted zero takedowns in all three of his last fights. A career 82% takedown defense is still meaningful, but his offensive wrestling — once a legitimate threat at 2.11 per 15 minutes — has gone entirely dormant.
- At 36, in the welterweight-to-lightweight age range, he is past the threshold where decline typically accelerates. He has also missed weight twice in his career, and the pattern of early stoppages suggests his body may be carrying the accumulated cost of a long, hard career at a brutal weight class.
- His path back requires him to reinvent himself as a pure counter-striker or clinch-and-grind specialist — but there is no recent evidence he has done that. He beat Renato Moicano on points in a low-output decision, which is about the ceiling of what this version of Dariush appears capable of.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Quillan Salkilld -- Style & Profile

- Salkilld is a 26-year-old Australian fighting in front of his home crowd in Perth, and the stats reveal a genuinely two-dimensional fighter — not just a brawler. He averages 5.16 significant strikes per minute with 57% accuracy, and in the Mullarkey fight he shot 9.89 takedowns per 15 minutes. He can hurt you standing and take you down when he wants.
- His three-fight run shows sharp game-plan variation: a pure wrestling submission win over Mullarkey, a clean KO of Nasrat Haqparast (a solid UFC veteran) with his hands, and a decision win over Ashmouz using a blend of both. That adaptability is a real trait, not a coincidence.
- The KO of Haqparast is the most relevant data point here. Haqparast was a durable, experienced UFC lightweight — not a regional journeyman. Salkilld walked him down and put him away cleanly at 2:30 of the first round. That result speaks to real power and real timing at this level.
- His strike defense sits at 44%, which is below average — he absorbs 3.24 significant strikes per minute. Against elite strikers, that number would be a concern. Against a 36-year-old coming off consecutive first-round knockouts who has thrown almost no offense in his last two fights, it matters far less.
- His takedown defense is 90%, which means even if Dariush rediscovers his grappling, Salkilld is well-equipped to keep it standing. He fought his last three fights 90 days, a few months, and a few months apart — he is active, sharp, and not carrying ring rust.
- This is only his fifth UFC fight, so the sample is still limited. His one career loss came before his UFC run, and he has looked progressively sharper with each appearance. Home crowd in Perth adds a further layer of confidence and energy.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central problem for Dariush in this matchup is that Salkilld beats him in almost every category that matters right now. Salkilld is longer (3-inch reach advantage), younger by a decade, more active, and — critically — the man who walks forward and initiates. Dariush has historically been a forward-pressure fighter himself, but recent fights show him operating in survival mode rather than as an aggressor. When two fighters who both want to move forward meet, the one with more physical tools and fresher legs usually sets the pace. That's Salkilld.

The style question that would normally dominate a lightweight matchup — can the wrestler force it to the ground? — is answered pretty cleanly here. Dariush attempted zero takedowns across his last three fights and has shown no offensive grappling in over two years of fight tape. Salkilld's 90% takedown defense means even a theoretical Dariush grappling resurgence is unlikely to pay off. Dariush's best realistic path is a counter-striking performance — using his southpaw angle, his 49% striking accuracy, and the experience edge to catch Salkilld with something clean. It has happened in his career; the KO of Holtzman and Klose showed legitimate power when he was right. But the sequence of three R1 knockouts is not a run of bad luck. His chin has been compromised by accumulated damage, and Salkilld's power has been demonstrated against a credible UFC opponent.

The net picture favors Salkilld significantly. Dariush's only genuine edge is experience — 23 pro fights, multiple top-10 opponents, a southpaw stance that Salkilld has not faced repeatedly in his short career. If Dariush lands first and catches Salkilld looking for the takedown or walking in recklessly, there is a path. But the probabilities say that what is more likely is Salkilld walking Dariush down, using his reach to land before Dariush can counter, and either stopping him on the feet or grinding him out when Dariush's offense fails to materialize.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Quillan Salkilld wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or Round 2**
Likelihood: High
Why: The blueprint for beating Dariush right now is walk him down and hit him first. Salkilld has the forward pressure, the power (proven against Haqparast), and a 44% defense that suggests he won't try to be clever — he'll bang. With Dariush's chin having absorbed three first-round stoppages in his last four fights, and his ability to generate offense at a historic low, the finish probability here is real. Salkilld's best weapons (pressure striking, clean power shots) match perfectly against Dariush's established vulnerabilities.

**#2 -- Quillan Salkilld wins by decision**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Dariush's southpaw angles and experience neutralize some of Salkilld's power — keeping him on the outside, circling off the fence — this could extend to the cards. Salkilld doesn't need to finish to win, and even if Dariush survives the early rounds, his output has been so low in recent fights that winning on the scorecards would require a dramatic shift in approach. Three rounds of Salkilld outworking a defense-first Dariush is a plausible and clean decision win for Salkilld.

**#3 -- Quillan Salkilld wins by submission, Round 1**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Salkilld showed against Mullarkey that he can take a fight to the mat and finish with submissions — he posted nearly 10 takedowns per 15 minutes in that fight. If Dariush gets hurt on the feet and shoots desperately for a takedown to survive, Salkilld could reverse or capitalize on a scramble. This is a genuine if secondary path given his submission win on the card.

**#4 -- Beneil Dariush wins by KO/TKO or decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: The Moicano win showed Dariush can still survive and outpoint an opponent who lets him settle into a rhythm. If Salkilld walks in recklessly early and Dariush catches him with a southpaw left hand counter — his best historical weapon — a finish or a points victory is not impossible. It requires Salkilld to fight carelessly and Dariush to recapture timing and output that hasn't been present in over two years of fighting.
analysis_sections.betting:
**Tier: PLAY**
**Primary Pick: Quillan Salkilld — Confidence: 74%**

Salkilld is priced between -350 and -455 across books, with the best available line being -350 at BetUS and BetOnline. Dariush's best price is +330 at BetRivers and FanDuel.

Converting the best available lines to implied probabilities: Salkilld at -350 implies 77.8%, Dariush at +330 implies 23.3% — combined that's 101.1%, a standard vig. De-vigging: Salkilld's true market probability sits around 77.0%, Dariush's around 23.0%.

The assessed probability for Salkilld here is approximately 74%. That is slightly *below* the market's de-vigged 77%, which means there is no positive edge on Salkilld at the current price — the market has him appropriately priced or slightly overpriced given the matchup.

**Recommendation: NO BET on Salkilld at -350 or shorter.**

The read on who wins this fight is not in doubt — Salkilld should win, and probably finishes it. But paying -350 for a fighter in a fight that still carries meaningful upset risk (Dariush is a 36-year-old veteran with power and a southpaw awkwardness edge) doesn't produce value. At -350, you need to be right more than 77.8% of the time just to break even, and the honest assessed probability here sits below that line.

**On Dariush at +330 (BetRivers/FanDuel):** The implied probability is 23.3%, de-vigged to roughly 23.0%. The assessed probability for Dariush is approximately 26% — a narrow gap of about 3 percentage points above the market's true price. That is on the edge of the noise threshold. At +330, the value case just barely exists mathematically, but it rests almost entirely on a single path: Dariush lands a counter shot early, and Salkilld — who is still a young, relatively inexperienced fighter with 44% strike defense — walks into it. That is a real possibility; it is not a solid foundation for a bet.

## Beneil Dariush -- Style & Profile

- Dariush built his reputation as one of the more complete lightweights in the UFC — a wrestler with sharp hands, good submission awareness, and the kind of patient pressure that wore down Tony Ferguson and Mateusz Gamrot. That version of him, however, is increasingly difficult to find in recent fight footage.
- He has been knocked out in the first round in three of his last four fights — by Charles Oliveira (R1, 4:10), Arman Tsarukyan (R1, 1:04), and most recently Benoit Saint Denis (R1, 0:16). That 16-second knockout is the clearest signal of all: he is being stopped earlier each time, by opponents with varying levels of KO power.
- His striking output has collapsed. Career average is 3.78 significant strikes per minute, but his last three fights show 11.25 (a brawl he got stopped in), 1.47, and 1.88 — the two wins/survivals came from volume suppression and survival mode, not output. His accuracy also dropped to 25% against Tsarukyan, suggesting his timing is deteriorating.
- The grappling that made him dangerous has completely disappeared from recent footage. He attempted zero takedowns in all three of his last fights. A career 82% takedown defense is still meaningful, but his offensive wrestling — once a legitimate threat at 2.11 per 15 minutes — has gone entirely dormant.
- At 36, in the welterweight-to-lightweight age range, he is past the threshold where decline typically accelerates. He has also missed weight twice in his career, and the pattern of early stoppages suggests his body may be carrying the accumulated cost of a long, hard career at a brutal weight class.
- His path back requires him to reinvent himself as a pure counter-striker or clinch-and-grind specialist — but there is no recent evidence he has done that. He beat Renato Moicano on points in a low-output decision, which is about the ceiling of what this version of Dariush appears capable of.
<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Quillan Salkilld -- Style & Profile

- Salkilld is a 26-year-old Australian fighting in front of his home crowd in Perth, and the stats reveal a genuinely two-dimensional fighter — not just a brawler. He averages 5.16 significant strikes per minute with 57% accuracy, and in the Mullarkey fight he shot 9.89 takedowns per 15 minutes. He can hurt you standing and take you down when he wants.
- His three-fight run shows sharp game-plan variation: a pure wrestling submission win over Mullarkey, a clean KO of Nasrat Haqparast (a solid UFC veteran) with his hands, and a decision win over Ashmouz using a blend of both. That adaptability is a real trait, not a coincidence.
- The KO of Haqparast is the most relevant data point here. Haqparast was a durable, experienced UFC lightweight — not a regional journeyman. Salkilld walked him down and put him away cleanly at 2:30 of the first round. That result speaks to real power and real timing at this level.
- His strike defense sits at 44%, which is below average — he absorbs 3.24 significant strikes per minute. Against elite strikers, that number would be a concern. Against a 36-year-old coming off consecutive first-round knockouts who has thrown almost no offense in his last two fights, it matters far less.
- His takedown defense is 90%, which means even if Dariush rediscovers his grappling, Salkilld is well-equipped to keep it standing. He fought his last three fights 90 days, a few months, and a few months apart — he is active, sharp, and not carrying ring rust.
- This is only his fifth UFC fight, so the sample is still limited. His one career loss came before his UFC run, and he has looked progressively sharper with each appearance. Home crowd in Perth adds a further layer of confidence and energy.

## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central problem for Dariush in this matchup is that Salkilld beats him in almost every category that matters right now. Salkilld is longer (3-inch reach advantage), younger by a decade, more active, and — critically — the man who walks forward and initiates. Dariush has historically been a forward-pressure fighter himself, but recent fights show him operating in survival mode rather than as an aggressor. When two fighters who both want to move forward meet, the one with more physical tools and fresher legs usually sets the pace. That's Salkilld.

The style question that would normally dominate a lightweight matchup — can the wrestler force it to the ground? — is answered pretty cleanly here. Dariush attempted zero takedowns across his last three fights and has shown no offensive grappling in over two years of fight tape. Salkilld's 90% takedown defense means even a theoretical Dariush grappling resurgence is unlikely to pay off. analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Tim Elliott -- Style & Profile

- Elliott is a grappler-first fighter whose real weapon is relentless forward pressure and a constant search for the takedown or clinch. His 3.71 takedowns per 15 minutes is elite for flyweight, and he backs that up with 1.1 submission attempts per 15 minutes — he doesn't just want the takedown, he wants to finish from it. His last two wins were both submissions.
- The striking is functional but limited. He's averaging 3.37 significant strikes per minute over his UFC career but has zero knockout or TKO wins in his last 10 fights. He throws to set up grappling entries, not to hurt people. His 44% accuracy reflects busy, purposeful output rather than surgical precision.
- His last-3 striking volume has dropped sharply — 1.66, 2.48, and 1.53 strikes per minute in those three fights versus a career average of 3.37. That's not a stylistic shift; it suggests he's fought opponents (Mokaev, Asakura) who disrupted his rhythm, and his offense is becoming increasingly dependent on grappling to generate finishes.
- At 39, he's well past the age cliff for men his size, and he carries two weight misses in his history — a warning sign about his body's relationship with the 125-pound limit. His chin has never been the issue (no KO losses in recent memory), but his athleticism and explosiveness are the real concern at this stage of his career.
- He's been active recently — the Asakura win in August 2025 was a solid, late submission in Round 2, which shows he can still close shows against live opponents. That fight and the Sumudaerji win before it suggest his submission game remains sharp even if his striking has quieted.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Steve Erceg -- Style & Profile

- Erceg is a volume striker on paper — 4.00 significant strikes per minute career average — but his last three fights tell a different story. He put up 0.2, 1.0, and 2.21 strikes per minute respectively, with his accuracy sitting at 27%, 37%, and 31% across those outings. That is a dramatic collapse in offensive output, and it's the most important number in this fight.
- His defensive numbers are genuinely concerning. He absorbs 3.79 significant strikes per minute over his career, and his 52% strike defense is below average for the division. The Kai Kara-France loss was a Round 1 KO in under five minutes, and the losses to Pantoja and Moreno were both clear decision defeats across five rounds — he has struggled to impose himself against anyone ranked above him.
- He was knocked out by Kai Kara-France in Round 1, and that KO came from a fighter with a proven record of finishing people. He has been knocked out once and now carries that on his ledger. The Ode Osbourne decision in his most recent fight — where he managed only 0.2 strikes per minute with 27% accuracy — is difficult to explain as anything other than a severely diminished offensive fighter.
- His grappling is essentially not a weapon. A 27% takedown accuracy rate on 1.15 attempts per 15 minutes means he is not a credible wrestling threat, and with 0.4 submission attempts per 15 minutes and zero submission wins in his last seven fights, the ground game is off the table offensively.
- He is the hometown fighter, born in Perth and fighting in front of his home crowd. That's a real if modest boost in terms of crowd energy and atmosphere, but it doesn't change what the stats show about his recent trajectory.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central matchup question here is whether Erceg can prevent Elliott from dictating where the fight takes place — and the numbers say he probably can't. Elliott averages 3.71 takedowns per 15 minutes with 47% accuracy and a grappling-first approach that has produced his last two finishes. Erceg's takedown defense sits at 62%, which is adequate but not elite, and critically, his recent performance data shows he is not generating the offensive output to keep a pressure grappler honest. When a striker can't produce volume and can't threaten the takedown himself, a relentless takedown-heavy opponent is going to find openings. Elliott's path is straightforward: pressure forward, mix in level changes, get the fight to the mat, and work submissions from top position or off his back.

Erceg's path to winning runs through his striking at range and his ability to time Elliott's takedown entries. Elliott's 59% takedown defense is not dominant — he does get taken down himself — but Erceg has not demonstrated the wrestling or grappling to exploit that. The more realistic version of Erceg winning is outpointing Elliott on the feet across three rounds, keeping his distance, using his 2-inch reach advantage, and making the fight ugly enough in the clinch to steal rounds. The problem is his last three fights show he is simply not landing at the volume or accuracy needed to do that. Against a 39-year-old Elliott, the reflexive lean is toward Erceg, but the recent fight data fundamentally undermines the assumption that he is the sharper striker right now.

The intangibles cut against both fighters to some degree — both are coming off layoffs just under nine months — but the age gap is the sharpest differential here. Elliott is 39 fighting a 30-year-old, and the athleticism and explosiveness questions are real. At the same time, Elliott's game is built on pressure and grappling technique rather than speed and power, which tends to age better than pure striking. Erceg's recent KO loss and his dramatic offensive decline make him a much weaker favorite than the -170 to -190 market suggests, and Elliott's submission-finishing ability gives him a genuine path to ending the fight.
analysis_sections.endings:

**#1 -- Tim Elliott wins by submission, Round 2 or 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: Elliott has finished his last two opponents by submission, and Erceg's grappling defense has never been tested at a high level against a specialist of Elliott's quality. Elliott's pressure style is designed to accumulate control time and create submission opportunities, and Erceg's inability to generate offensive output in his last three fights means he won't be landing the strikes needed to discourage the takedown attempts.

**#2 -- Steve Erceg wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Erceg can use his reach, fight smart on the outside, and deny enough takedown attempts over three rounds, the judges may reward him for the cleaner striking even at low volume. Elliott's age and the stylistic difficulty of catching a patient counter-fighter could lead to a work-for-hire decision where Erceg gets credit for moments without ever truly controlling the fight.

**#3 -- Tim Elliott wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Moderate-Low
Why: If Elliott can't finish — Erceg has not been stopped by anything other than the Kara-France KO — Elliott's pressure, takedown accumulation, and aggression could be enough to take rounds from a fighter who is producing 0.2 to 1.0 strikes per minute in recent outings. Three rounds of forward pace against a passive striker could read clearly on the scorecards.

**#4 -- Steve Erceg wins by KO/TKO, Round 1**
Likelihood: Low
Why: The Kara-France win over Schnell showed Erceg can generate knockout power when things break right, and Elliott at 39 walking into a clean shot isn't impossible. Elliott has no KO losses on record which pushes this scenario toward the low end, but it exists as a live possibility given how aggressively Elliott pressures forward.
analysis_sections.betting:
## Betting Recommendation

**The Pick: Tim Elliott**

The market has Elliott priced as a significant underdog — anywhere from +142 to +158 depending on the book, with the best available line at +158 on FanDuel. That implies the market thinks he wins roughly 38-39% of the time at face value. The de-vigged true probability on Elliott across the combined market lands around 37-38%.

The problem with that number is what Erceg's recent fight data actually shows. A flyweight being listed as a -170 to -190 favorite while posting 0.2 strikes per minute in his most recent win, going 0-2 in his last two real tests, absorbing a Round 1 KO, and carrying essentially no grappling offense — that fighter is not a 62-63% win probability against a submission specialist with back-to-back finishing wins. The market is pricing Erceg's pre-2024 version, not the fighter who showed up in the last three outings.

Elliott's case is built on: two recent submission finishes against live opponents, a grappling volume and finishing rate that gives him genuine win equity, and a style (pressure wrestling, technical submission hunting) that ages better than power striking. Erceg cannot stop takedowns reliably at 62% defense, cannot generate offensive output to deter them, and is coming off a KO loss that is now on his record. Elliott is nine years older, which is a real concern, but his path to winning doesn't require athleticism — it requires relentlessness, and that hasn't left him.

The assessed win probability for Elliott is approximately 50-52%, which is well above what the market is crediting him. The best line is +158 on FanDuel.

**Line:** Tim Elliott +158 (FanDuel)
**Implied probability (raw):** ~38.8%
**De-vigged true probability:** ~37.5%
**Assessed probability:** ~50%
**Stake:** 1.5 units
**Target line:** The bet has value down to approximately +115. Do not take Elliott shorter than +115.

**Data note:** Both fighters are returning from similar layoffs (~258-265 days), a roughly neutral factor. Elliott's two weight misses in his history are flagged but not recent enough to confirm an active weight issue. Erceg's intangibles data was unavailable from Tapology. The confidence here is built on the striking and grappling splits, not on soft factors.

**Market read:** The line has moved sharply toward Erceg — he opened around -158 and is now priced to -170/-190 depending on the book, while Elliott moved from +210 to his current range of +142 to +158. This is not reverse line movement; the market is confirming the sharp money is on Erceg. That is a real signal and it tempers confidence in this play. The smart read may be that Erceg's stylistic advantages or the home-crowd boost have been assessed more positively by sharps than the raw stats suggest. This does not flip the recommendation, but it does mean the play is sized conservatively rather than aggressively.

**Hedge verdict:** NO ARB. The best available line on Erceg is -170 (BetOnline.ag) and the best on Elliott is +158 (FanDuel). Combined implied probability is approximately 60.9% + 38.8% = 99.7% — there is no arbitrage available. Take the value on Elliott at +158 on FanDuel.

**Props worth considering:** There is potential value on "fight goes to decision — NO" given Elliott's two recent submission finishes and the general grapplings-specialist-vs-passive-striker dynamic. Erceg has not been submitted in his career but has also never faced a pure submission hunter of Elliott's caliber on the mat. If the prop is available at a reasonable line, it merits a look — but de-vig the market before committing.

**Flip conditions:** If Erceg's 62% takedown defense understates his actual ability to stay upright against Elliott's wrestling (e.g., better-than-average sprawl game not visible in the TD defense stat), the read weakens. If the Asakura win was padded by Asakura's own defensive lapses rather than Elliott's execution, recalibrate downward. If the sharp line movement toward Erceg is driven by camp intelligence or injury news not in the public data, the play should be abandoned.

Why: If Elliott can't finish — Erceg has not been stopped by anything other than the Kara-France KO — Elliott's pressure, takedown accumulation, and aggression could be enough to take rounds from a fighter who is producing 0.2 to 1.0 strikes per minute in recent outings. Three rounds of forward pace against a passive striker could read clearly on the scorecards.

**#4 -- Steve Erceg wins by KO/TKO, Round 1**
Likelihood: Low
Why: The Kara-France win over Schnell showed Erceg can generate knockout power when things break right, and Elliott at 39 walking into a clean shot isn't impossible. Elliott has no KO losses on record which pushes this scenario toward the low end, but it exists as a live possibility given how aggressively Elliott pressures forward.

<!--END-->

<!--BETTING-->
## Betting Recommendation

**The Pick: Tim Elliott**

The market has Elliott priced as a significant underdog — anywhere from +142 to +158 depending on the book, with the best available line at +158 on FanDuel. That implies the market thinks he wins roughly 38-39% of the time at face value. The de-vigged true probability on Elliott across the combined market lands around 37-38%.

The problem with that number is what Erceg's recent fight data actually shows. A flyweight being listed as a -170 to -190 favorite while posting 0.2 strikes per minute in his most recent win, going 0-2 in his last two real tests, absorbing a Round 1 KO, and carrying essentially no grappling offense — that fighter is not a 62-63% win probability against a submission specialist with back-to-back finishing wins. The market is pricing Erceg's pre-2024 version, not the fighter who showed up in the last three outings.

Elliott's case is built on: two recent submission finishes against live opponents, a grappling volume and finishing rate that gives him genuine win equity, and a style (pressure wrestling, technical submission hunting) that ages better than power striking. Erceg cannot stop takedowns reliably at 62% defense, cannot generate offensive output to deter them, and is coming off a KO loss that is now on his record. Elliott is nine years older, which is a real concern, but his path to winning doesn't require athleticism — it requires relentlessness, and that hasn't left him.

The assessed win probability for Elliott is approximately 50-52%, which is well above what the market is crediting him. The best line is +158 on FanDuel.

**Line:** Tim Elliott +158 (FanDuel)
**Implied probability (raw):** ~38.8%
**De-vigged true probability:** ~37.5%
**Assessed probability:** ~50%
**Stake:** 1.5 units
**Target line:** The bet has value down to approximately +115. Do not take Elliott shorter than +115.

**Data note:** Both fighters are returning from similar layoffs (~258-265 days), a roughly neutral factor. Elliott's two weight misses in his history are flagged but not recent enough to confirm an active weight issue. Erceg's intangibles data was unavailable from Tapology. The confidence here is built on the striking and grappling splits, not on soft factors.

**Market read:** The line has moved sharply toward Erceg — he opened around -158 and is now priced to -170/-190 depending on the book, while Elliott moved from +210 to his current range of +142 to +158. This is not reverse line movement; the market is confirming the sharp money is on Erceg. That is a real signal and it tempers confidence in this play. The smart read may be that Erceg's stylistic advantages or the home-crowd boost have been assessed more positively by sharps than the raw stats suggest. This does not flip the recommendation, but it does mean the play is sized conservatively rather than aggressively.

**Hedge verdict:** NO ARB. The best available line on Erceg is -170 (BetOnline.ag) and the best on Elliott is +158 (FanDuel). Combined implied probability is approximately 60.9% + 38.8% = 99.7% — there is no arbitrage available. Take the value on Elliott at +158 on FanDuel.

**Props worth considering:** There is potential value on "fight goes to decision — NO" given Elliott's two recent submission finishes and the general grapplings-specialist-vs-passive-striker dynamic. Erceg has not been submitted in his career but has also never faced a pure submission hunter of Elliott's caliber on the mat. If the prop is available at a reasonable line, it merits a look — but de-vig the market before committing.

**Flip conditions:** If Erceg's 62% takedown defense understates his actual ability to stay upright against Elliott's wrestling (e.g., better-than-average sprawl game not visible in the TD defense stat), the read weakens. If the Asakura win was padded by Asakura's own defensive lapses rather than Elliott's execution, recalibrate downward. If the sharp line movement toward Erceg is driven by camp intelligence or injury news not in the public data, the play should be abandoned.

<!--END-->

## Marwan Rahiki -- Style & Profile

- At 23 years old and 8-0, Rahiki is the most active fighter on this card with the shortest turnaround — 48 days since his last finish. He's operating at the pace of a fighter who knows exactly what he is right now.
- His UFC output is built around a high-volume striking game: 7.73 significant strikes landed per minute is elite-level output, well above the featherweight average. He does not look to wrestle — zero takedown attempts in his UFC career — so everything runs through his hands.
- The absorption number is the thing that demands attention. He takes 6.33 significant strikes per minute, which is genuinely alarming for a fighter at this level. His 56% strike defense means opponents are landing on him at a high rate, and he is apparently content to trade rather than avoid.
- Both UFC wins came in Round 2 by TKO, both against fighters he clearly outgunned. His accuracy has ticked up slightly fight-to-fight (41% vs. Mulumba, 53% vs. Hardwick), suggesting he's getting sharper with more UFC experience.
- He has yet to be seriously tested on the chin. The absorption rate he's carrying into this fight would be a major concern against a quality power striker, but there's no evidence yet of how he responds to being hurt.
- With only two UFC fights on the books, the sample is thin. What we know is a finisher who walks through fire to throw volume — which works until it doesn't.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Ollie Schmid -- Style & Profile

- Schmid is a UFC debutant with a 4-2 professional record, and the data situation is stark: zero UFC stats exist, no fight history was parsed, and no Tapology intangibles are available. Every observation here is grounded in the structural fact that this is his first UFC appearance.
- The 6'0" frame at featherweight gives him a notable height advantage over Rahiki (5'8"), though the reach gap is negligible at one inch. His size could give him leverage in the clinch and force Rahiki to work up through his guard, but that's speculative without confirmed stylistic data.
- His 4-2 record at the regional/international level is modest. Two losses before a UFC contract suggests he was not dominant on the regional circuit — he earned his way in but was not unbeaten when called up.
- Without fight tape or parsed history, his finishing tendencies, defensive habits, and response to pressure are genuinely unknown. That's not a knock — it's an honest accounting of what the data allows.
- First-time UFC fighters face a documented adjustment: cage geometry, glove differences, opponent quality jump, and fight-night nerves all pull against a debut performance matching their regional ceiling.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central problem for Schmid is that he's walking into a buzz saw with no UFC film to lean on. Rahiki throws volume at a rate most featherweights can't match — north of 7.5 significant strikes per minute in his UFC fights — and he finishes. Both UFC wins came by TKO in the second round. Schmid, who has no verified UFC stats and a modest pre-UFC record, has no established evidence of the chin, defensive athleticism, or countering ability needed to survive that kind of output long enough to impose his own game.

Rahiki's genuine vulnerability is his own defensive sloppiness. Absorbing 6.33 strikes per minute means he is a two-way fighter by default rather than by design — he gets hit a lot. If Schmid has meaningful power and the composure to capitalize on Rahiki walking onto shots, there's a path to an upset through a counter finish. The height advantage could also help Schmid establish range early and make Rahiki work harder to close distance. The problem is we have no evidence Schmid is that kind of finisher, and Rahiki's UFC opponents have not had time to figure out those vulnerabilities before the fight gets stopped.

The net intangible picture tilts heavily toward Rahiki — more UFC experience, a short and active layoff keeping him sharp, and a finish rate that speaks for itself. Schmid carries the standard debut discount and the fog of no available data. This is a fight where the structure is clear even without Schmid's full profile: Rahiki is the more tested, more active, more proven finisher, and his path to victory runs through the same place his last two wins did — high volume, pressure, and a Round 2 TKO.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 — Marwan Rahiki wins by TKO, Round 1 or Round 2**
Likelihood: High
Why: Rahiki has finished both UFC opponents in the second round by TKO, and he does it by walking opponents down with volume until the damage accumulates. A debuting fighter absorbing that pace without proven UFC-level durability is the exact scenario this pattern fits. The fight likely ends before the scorecards become relevant.

**#2 — Marwan Rahiki wins by decision**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Schmid's size and movement keep him out of serious danger early and Rahiki's accuracy dips (he was at 41% in his first UFC fight), this could go the distance with Rahiki winning clearly on volume. Less likely given Rahiki's finishing pattern, but the most realistic non-finish outcome for Rahiki.

**#3 — Ollie Schmid wins by KO/TKO**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Rahiki absorbs a significant amount of contact — 6.33 strikes per minute — and has not been tested by a fighter who can punish that with clean, powerful counters. If Schmid is a credible power striker with the composure to time Rahiki walking in, there is a real ceiling shot available. We simply have no data confirming Schmid is that fighter.
analysis_sections.betting:
**Recommendation: PASS on the Rahiki moneyline. No bet at current pricing.**

Rahiki is the correct fighter to be backing in this fight — that is not the question. The question is whether the price offers any value, and at -625 on the best available line, the math is unambiguous.

At -625, the implied probability is approximately 86.2%. De-vigged against Schmid's best available price of +530 (implied 15.9%), the market's true probability on Rahiki lands around 84.4%. To find any meaningful edge, the assessed win probability would need to exceed that — which requires high confidence against a debuting opponent with no UFC data. Rahiki is a real finisher with real problems (absorbing 6.33 strikes per minute against a fighter whose power and style are completely unknown), and while he should win this fight, "should win" at 84% confidence does not generate a bet at this price. The risk is still there. It is just priced as if it isn't.

**The dog side deserves a look.** Schmid at +530 on FanDuel implies roughly 15.9% true probability. For Schmid to represent value, his actual win probability needs to exceed that. Given that Rahiki leaks significant contact, has never been tested at a meaningful level, and is facing a taller opponent whose style is unknown, a Schmid win probability somewhere in the 18-22% range is defensible — but that's a soft read built on inference rather than data, which limits how aggressively you can act on it.

**Stake:** No bet on Rahiki moneyline. If taking a speculative shot on Schmid at +530, treat it as a small recreational play only — under 0.5 units — not a value play in the analytical sense.

**Target line:** Rahiki would need to be priced somewhere around -300 or shorter to generate a legitimate value play. The current market is pricing him as a near-certainty; the actual fight does not support that level of certainty given the defensive leakiness and the unknowns around his opponent.

**Market read:** Rahiki opened around -900 and has drifted out to -800 across books, with Schmid opening at much longer odds and shortening slightly on some books. The line movement has gone *against* Rahiki, meaning sharp money has been coming in on Schmid — or at least pushing back on the favorite price. This is not confirmed reverse line movement, but the directional move (Rahiki lengthening from -900 to -800) is worth noting. It suggests the market is not fully comfortable with the extreme favorite price, which aligns with the opacity around Schmid's actual level.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB.** Best available Rahiki: -625 (implied 86.2%). Best available Schmid: +530 (implied 15.9%). Combined implied: 102.1%. No arb exists. The book is extracting standard vig on both sides.

**Props:** Without knowing Schmid's durability or finishing tendencies, a "fight ends inside the distance" prop (if available at a reasonable price) aligns with Rahiki's pattern — he has not gone to a decision in his UFC career. But without the prop lines available to de-vig, this is observational only. No prop play recommended.

**The honest summary:** Rahiki wins this fight more often than not. The market knows that and has priced it accordingly. There is no number here that generates clean value on the favorite, and the Schmid side is a soft speculation at best. The correct play is to watch this one.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Shamil Gaziev -- Style & Profile

- Gaziev is a slow-output, high-accuracy heavyweight who hunts for a single clean shot rather than swarming opponents with volume. His career rate of 2.62 significant strikes per minute is well below the heavyweight average, but his 47% accuracy suggests the punches he throws tend to land — he's a deliberate, patient striker who waits for openings rather than creating them with pressure.
- His chin has been tested and cracked. He has been stopped twice by knockout in his career, and his most recent fight — a 1:22 first-round KO loss to Waldo Cortes Acosta in November 2025 — came on short notice against a known power threat. More concerning: he was previously stopped by Jairzinho Rozenstruik in a late fourth-round finish, suggesting both early-round vulnerability and some history of fading under sustained pressure. Two KO losses in his last six fights, one of them in the first 82 seconds, is a profile worth taking seriously.
- His defensive numbers are a significant red flag. He absorbs 3.97 significant strikes per minute against a strike defense of just 45% — meaning opponents are consistently finding him with more than half the punches they throw. That combination of low output and porous defense leaves him in a dangerous spot against a violent finisher.
- Gaziev's wrestling actually serves him decently as a survival tool. His 83% takedown defense means he's unlikely to end up on the floor against anyone who's not a dedicated grappler, and his one takedown attempt per 15 minutes shows he'll occasionally shoot to change the fight's dynamics — though his 30% accuracy on those attempts means they rarely convert.
- At 36, he's past the age threshold where heavyweights begin showing meaningful chin and athletic decline. The chin decay signs are already present in his recent fight history — this is a fighter whose window may have closed before the public line has fully adjusted.
- His last-3 striking volume has jumped sharply from his career average (4.39 and 4.38 SLpM in his last two active fights vs. 2.62 career), but his accuracy dropped in both of those fights. More volume at lower accuracy is a sign he's been forced into uncomfortable exchanges rather than picking his spots — a pattern that tends to get punished against elite finishers.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Brando Pericic -- Style & Profile

- Pericic's striking numbers are genuinely extraordinary — 12.91 significant strikes per minute at 64% accuracy across his UFC career. To put that in context, most elite volume strikers in the heavyweight division sit around 5–7 SLpM. Pericic is throwing more than twice that, and he's landing nearly two-thirds of them. Whether those numbers hold against stiffer competition is the central question, but on their face they're remarkable.
- Both UFC finishes came inside two minutes of the first round, against Elisha Ellison (1:55) and Louie Sutherland (1:48). He doesn't win fights — he ends them. His most recent last-3 shows 10.56 SLpM at 73% accuracy against Sutherland, suggesting the volume-and-accuracy combination isn't just early-fight desperation; it's a sustained weapon.
- His defensive numbers are solid for someone who fights at this pace — 55% strike defense and only 3.23 absorbed per minute. That's a fighter who isn't just running into punches while throwing his own; he has some sense of head movement or range control, though the sample size on his UFC career is still thin.
- The glaring unknown is durability. He has never been seriously tested or hurt in the UFC. With only 2 UFC fights and no rounds that have gone past the 1:55 mark, we simply don't know what his chin looks like under sustained fire or what his cardio looks like past the opening exchanges. That uncertainty cuts both ways — he could be an elite finisher or a padded-record phenomenon.
- He's 31, physically fresh at 245 lbs on a 41-day turnaround, and has shown no weight or camp red flags in the available data. The short turnaround from his last fight is worth monitoring but is not inherently a problem for a fighter who hasn't taken serious damage in recent outings.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The core tension in this fight is a genuinely alarming striker in Pericic against a heavyweight who has been knocked out twice, absorbs shots at a high rate, and is five years past his athletic prime. Pericic brings 12.91 significant strikes per minute — a rate that suggests he will immediately impose a pace Gaziev has never faced. Gaziev's 45% strike defense means Pericic should land on roughly half his attempts. In a fight between two pure strikers with no meaningful grappling threat from either side (Pericic has zero takedown attempts in his UFC career; Gaziev's takedowns convert at 30%), this will almost certainly be decided by who lands clean first.

The problem for Gaziev is the structural setup. He is a slow-output, low-defense fighter who has been violently stopped twice in recent memory, including a first-round knockout in his last outing. He is 36, which for a heavyweight marks the beginning of real physical decline. His recent trend of throwing more volume while landing less accurately suggests he's been reactive and uncomfortable, not sharp and confident. Against Pericic's pace, he will need to land a clean, heavy shot early — and he does have the power to do so. His two recent KO wins over Thomas Petersen and Martin Buday show legitimate finishing power when his timing is on. If Gaziev can weather the opening storm and land one of his cleaner right hands, Pericic's untested chin becomes the question.

Pericic's path is much simpler and more probable: come out at the pace he always fights, find the rhythm quickly, and land before Gaziev can set his feet for a counter. The real hedge on Pericic is the unknowns — he has never been in a real fight yet in the UFC. He has finished men who were outmatched and unremarkable. Gaziev, for all his recent struggles, is a veteran who has faced Jairzinho Rozenstruik and Waldo Cortes Acosta. The leap in competition is real. The line has tightened considerably from Gaziev opening as a significant favorite, which reflects genuine market uncertainty — but the structural read still points toward Pericic's pace and aggression being too much for a 36-year-old with a recently-compromised chin.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Brando Pericic wins by KO/TKO, Round 1**
Likelihood: High
Why: Pericic has finished both UFC opponents inside two minutes with an extraordinary volume and accuracy combination. Gaziev's 45% strike defense and two recent knockout losses give Pericic exactly the type of opponent his style exploits. If Pericic comes out at his normal pace, the math strongly favors an early finish before Gaziev can settle into his patient counterpunching game.

**#2 -- Shamil Gaziev wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate-Low
Why: Gaziev does carry genuine finishing power — he's stopped six opponents in his career, including a first-round KO over Thomas Petersen in February 2025. If Pericic's aggression gets him walking onto a clean right hand in the early rounds, Pericic's untested chin is the great unknown. Gaziev's best chance is a counter shot while Pericic is loading up.

**#3 -- Brando Pericic wins by KO/TKO, Round 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Gaziev somehow survives the early storm and the fight extends into round two, Pericic's pace and freshness still favor him. Gaziev has shown a pattern of absorbing damage and fading — the Rozenstruik fight went four rounds before a late stoppage. A round-two finish is the secondary scenario if Gaziev's durability holds past the opening minutes.

**#4 -- Shamil Gaziev wins by decision**
Likelihood: Very Low
Why: Gaziev did win a unanimous decision over Don'Tale Mayes in August 2024, so he can grind out rounds when he controls pace and keeps the exchanges measured. However, Pericic's volume and forward pressure make it extremely difficult to envision Gaziev outpointing him over 15 minutes without a dramatic stylistic shift. This outcome requires Pericic to gas out or become hesitant, neither of which is supported by his recent fights.
analysis_sections.betting:
**Recommendation: LEAN — Brando Pericic**
Confidence: 59%

The best available line on Pericic is +100 at DraftKings and BetMGM. At even money, the implied probability is 50%. After removing the book's margin, the market's true probability on Pericic sits at approximately 47.5–48.5% depending on the book. The assessed win probability for Pericic, based on the structural matchup, is approximately 59% — a meaningful gap, particularly at plus-money.

That said, this is a lean, not a play, and the reasons are important. Pericic has exactly two UFC fights. Both opponents were finishing fodder and neither provided a real test of his chin, his gas tank, or his ability to adjust mid-fight. The market clearly moved off Gaziev hard — he opened at -155 and is now down to -120 across most books, reflecting genuine doubt about his viability after the Cortes Acosta knockout. The line movement is in Pericic's direction without being driven by clear sharp action reversing the public. This looks more like a market re-rating Gaziev downward than sharps hammering Pericic. Both of those reads point the same way, but neither provides the kind of high-conviction signal that would justify a full play on a 2-fight UFC newcomer.

**Stake: 1.0 unit at Brando Pericic +100 (DraftKings or BetMGM)**
**Target line: No worse than -110.** If the line on Pericic moves past -110 before fight time, the value largely disappears and the recommendation downgrades to a pass.

**Data context:** Gaziev has a UFC sample sufficient to draw conclusions from; Pericic's two-fight UFC sample means all conclusions about him carry meaningful uncertainty. The Pericic analysis is heavily dependent on his striking numbers reflecting a genuine attribute rather than a product of soft opposition — if his accuracy collapses against a veteran like Gaziev, the whole structural read changes.

**Flip conditions:** If Pericic's striking accuracy against Sutherland (73.1%) is revealed to be anomalous and his prior opponents had notable defensive gaps, the read weakens. If Gaziev's KO loss to Cortes Acosta was heavily context-dependent (short notice, bad camp), some of the chin-decay concern softens. Neither of these data points is currently available.

## Shamil Gaziev -- Style & Profile

- Gaziev is a slow-output, high-accuracy heavyweight who hunts for a single clean shot rather than swarming opponents with volume. His career rate of 2.62 significant strikes per minute is well below the heavyweight average, but his 47% accuracy suggests the punches he throws tend to land — he's a deliberate, patient striker who waits for openings rather than creating them with pressure.
- His chin has been tested and cracked. He has been stopped twice by knockout in his career, and his most recent fight — a 1:22 first-round KO loss to Waldo Cortes Acosta in November 2025 — came on short notice against a known power threat. More concerning: he was previously stopped by Jairzinho Rozenstruik in a late fourth-round finish, suggesting both early-round vulnerability and some history of fading under sustained pressure. Two KO losses in his last six fights, one of them in the first 82 seconds, is a profile worth taking seriously.
- His defensive numbers are a significant red flag. He absorbs 3.97 significant strikes per minute against a strike defense of just 45% — meaning opponents are consistently finding him with more than half the punches they throw. That combination of low output and porous defense leaves him in a dangerous spot against a violent finisher.
- Gaziev's wrestling actually serves him decently as a survival tool. His 83% takedown defense means he's unlikely to end up on the floor against anyone who's not a dedicated grappler, and his one takedown attempt per 15 minutes shows he'll occasionally shoot to change the fight's dynamics — though his 30% accuracy on those attempts means they rarely convert.
- At 36, he's past the age threshold where heavyweights begin showing meaningful chin and athletic decline. The chin decay signs are already present in his recent fight history — this is a fighter whose window may have closed before the public line has fully adjusted.
- His last-3 striking volume has jumped sharply from his career average (4.39 and 4.38 SLpM in his last two active fights vs. 2.62 career), but his accuracy dropped in both of those fights. More volume at lower accuracy is a sign he's been forced into uncomfortable exchanges rather than picking his spots — a pattern that tends to get punished against elite finishers.

<!--F2_PROFILE-->

To put that in context, most elite volume strikers in the heavyweight division sit around 5–7 SLpM. Pericic is throwing more than twice that, and he's landing nearly two-thirds of them. ## Tai Tuivasa -- Style & Profile

- Tuivasa built his UFC reputation as a pure knockout artist — 4 KO/TKO wins in his last 10 fights, all finished inside two rounds. He has never attempted a takedown in his UFC career and has never won by any method other than KO/TKO. He is a one-dimensional power puncher, full stop.
- His striking numbers have cratered. Career average is 3.66 significant strikes per minute, but his last three fights show 0.13, 0.60, and 3.63 respectively — the first two are essentially non-functional output levels, consistent with a fighter who is no longer initiating and is getting out-worked without throwing back meaningfully.
- He absorbs an alarming volume of damage. Career SApM sits at 4.97, and his strike defense is 43% — meaning well over half of everything thrown at him lands. This is not a defensive fighter who neutralizes offense; he eats shots in exchange for the hope of landing something big.
- He is on a six-fight losing streak, and the quality of those losses is telling: finished by submission twice, knocked out, and decisioned three times. He has not looked like a threat in any of those fights. The chin that once carried him through exchanges appears genuinely compromised — he was knocked out by Pavlovich in 54 seconds and TKO'd late by Gane.
- His southpaw stance and power remain technically real weapons, but they are weapons that require forward pressure and timing — both of which have visibly deteriorated across his last four performances.
- At 33 with this much accumulated damage and a six-fight skid, there is very little evidence left that the fighter who knocked out Derrick Lewis still exists in any functional sense.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Louie Sutherland -- Style & Profile

- Sutherland has only two UFC fights on record, both losses — one by KO/TKO in Round 1 and one by submission in Round 1. His last-3 sample only contains two fights, which makes meaningful trend analysis unreliable.
- The most alarming number in his profile is his strike defense: 22% career average in the UFC. That means roughly 78% of incoming significant strikes land cleanly against him. For context, the UFC average strike defense hovers around 50–55%. Sutherland gets hit at an extraordinary rate.
- Despite the terrible defense, he has shown genuine activity when he can stay upright — 5.0 SLpM and 50% accuracy in his most recent fight, and a 8.33 takedowns-per-15-minutes showing that he can threaten the grappling game when the scramble opens up.
- He has missed weight twice in his career, which signals body composition and camp management issues. For a heavyweight competing against someone with genuine knockout power, a drained or compromised body on fight night is a meaningful concern.
- His two UFC losses both ended in Round 1 — one standing, one on the ground. There is no demonstrated evidence he can survive past the opening frame against opponents with legitimate finishing tools.
- Coming in on just 41 days of turnaround after a KO loss, he is returning from a stoppage defeat with minimal recovery time — fighters coming back quickly from knockouts often show the effects of that damage in their next outing.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This matchup is structurally simple and structurally ugly at the same time. Tuivasa is a power puncher who needs his opponent to stand in front of him and trade. Sutherland has 22% strike defense, which means he essentially does stand in front of people and trade — he just absorbs nearly everything thrown at him. On paper, that sounds like a gift for Tuivasa. And it probably is, if Tuivasa still has anything left.

The problem is that Tuivasa's output has collapsed. In two of his last three fights he was landing under one significant strike per minute — he is not a man who is hunting finishes anymore, he is a man who is surviving. Sutherland, whatever his defensive deficiencies, was landing 5.0 strikes per minute in his last fight and attempted 8+ takedowns per 15 minutes. In a fight between two deteriorating heavyweights, the man who still actually attacks has a meaningful edge. Tuivasa's takedown defense is 60% and he has never shown any grappling threat or takedown offense — if Sutherland can get him down, he likely controls him on the ground, though neither fighter has demonstrated submission finishing ability.

The net picture: Sutherland is a genuinely live underdog because Tuivasa no longer looks like a dangerous offensive fighter. But Sutherland's own track record in the UFC is two fights, two first-round losses, one of which was a KO with 22% strike defense against an incoming power puncher. If Tuivasa lands something meaningful early, Sutherland's defensive numbers suggest it almost certainly ends. This is a fight where the most predictable outcome is a sloppy early exchange that one man doesn't survive — and right now, Sutherland is the man with the worse defensive ceiling and the shorter UFC track record.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Cam Rowston -- Style & Profile

- Rowston is a forward-pressure striker who hunts the finish. Three straight stoppages — all inside two rounds — show a fighter whose power has become his defining weapon. He doesn't need volume to finish; he needs contact.
- His output varies wildly fight to fight: 8.76 strikes per minute against Holmes, then 2.52 against Brundage. That's not inconsistency for its own sake — it's a fighter who adjusts his pace to what the finish requires. He slows down when he can see the knockout coming.
- Accuracy has been inconsistent (42–50% in his last three, which is acceptable for a finisher), but his strike defense is a real concern: 51% career, and his takedown defense sits at just 33%. Anyone who can hold him against the fence or drag him to the mat can neutralize his offense entirely.
- His three-win streak came against credible but beatable opposition — Brundage, Petroski, and Holmes are solid UFC middleweights, not elite names. Still, three finishes in three fights is not nothing, and his timing looks sharp.
- At 31, he's on the right side of the age curve with 90 days off — fresh, no rust concerns, no late-career decline to price in. This is Rowston at or near his athletic peak.
- He's never been finished himself in his UFC run and owns a 3-inch reach advantage in this fight. He gets to set the range and dictate when and where contact happens.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Robert Bryczek -- Style & Profile

- Bryczek is a conservative, pressure-resistant fighter who absorbs more than he should. His career 4.08 significant strikes absorbed per minute is a worrying number for someone now at 35 — he invites contact rather than avoiding it.
- His 36% career striking accuracy is among the lower figures at middleweight, which means he needs volume or power to compensate for his imprecision. Against the Tavares fight, his accuracy jumped to 53% — his best output in the UFC — but that was against an aging Tavares who was slow and hittable. We don't know which Bryczek shows up here.
- The Potieria fight (0.80 strikes per minute, 34% accuracy, unanimous decision loss) paints a very different picture from the Tavares finish. He has a wide performance range, and his floor is worryingly low.
- His grappling data looks elite on paper — 85% takedown defense, 100% takedown accuracy — but this is built on an extremely small sample across just two UFC fights. Don't weight it like it's a proven quality.
- At 35, coming off a 237-day layoff, there are real ring rust concerns. He hasn't fought since September 2025, and the moderate layoff penalty applies — not catastrophic, but enough to nudge the math.
- He has been stopped twice in his career, including in the Potieria loss via decision after being dominated throughout. When he's outclassed on the feet, he doesn't find a way out — he just absorbs.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central tension in this fight is Rowston's power and finishing timing against Bryczek's durability and volume. Rowston is the sharper, more dangerous striker of the two — he's landing cleanly, finishing fights early, and operating with a 3-inch reach advantage that lets him pick his spots from the outside. Bryczek, by contrast, absorbs a lot of punishment (over 4 strikes per minute for his career) and relies on getting into a rhythm before he can do real damage. That's a dangerous game against someone who puts fighters down in the first two rounds.

Bryczek's path to winning runs almost entirely through surviving the early exchanges and grinding Rowston into a pace fight. If he can make it to the championship rounds while staying competitive on the feet, his slightly higher output and willingness to trade could wear Rowston down — Rowston's one UFC loss was a decision, suggesting he can be outworked over three rounds when the finish doesn't come. But Bryczek's 237-day layoff, his age (35), and his historically porous strike defense make it very difficult to trust he shows up as the Tavares-fight version rather than the Potieria-fight version. Rowston's takedown defense is legitimately poor at 33%, which is worth noting — if Bryczek chose to wrestle, he'd have a structural path there. But with 0 submission attempts across his UFC career, Bryczek has shown no interest in that game, so the fight is almost certain to stay on the feet.

The net intangible picture favors Rowston meaningfully. He's four years younger, on a short active layoff, and coming in on a hot streak of finishes. Bryczek brings moderate ring rust, an age-related concern at 35, and a history of looking completely neutralized when the opponent quality jumps. The reach advantage in Rowston's hands only amplifies how difficult this fight is for Bryczek — Rowston can find his shots from a distance where Bryczek's 36% accuracy becomes even less useful.

**#1 -- Cam Rowston wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: High
Why: Rowston has finished three straight opponents inside two rounds, and Bryczek absorbs over 4 strikes per minute for his career — he takes punishment without much defensive adjustment. **#2 -- Cam Rowston wins by decision**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Bryczek survives the early storm and manages to make the fight ugly and defensive, Rowston's volume drops (as it did in the Brundage fight) and a decision becomes plausible. Rowston's only UFC loss was a unanimous decision, so we know he can be ground down if the finish doesn't materialize. Three rounds of disciplined movement from Bryczek could get him there.

**#3 -- Robert Bryczek wins by KO/TKO, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: The Tavares finish proved Bryczek can stop people when the fight slows and his opponent tires late. If Rowston expends a lot of energy chasing the finish in rounds one and two without getting it, there's a scenario where he fades and Bryczek catches him clean. It's a narrow path, but Bryczek's only UFC win came exactly this way.

**#4 -- Robert Bryczek wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Bryczek's volume output can be very low (the Potieria fight showed 0.80 SLpM), and Rowston isn't a dead-weight opponent who will stand there and lose rounds without threatening. For Bryczek to win a decision he'd need to be the aggressive, accurate version from the Tavares fight for all three rounds, and the layoff and age make that a difficult ask.
