analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Junior Tafa -- Style & Profile

- Tafa is a straight-ahead pressure striker who lives and dies by his hands. He throws with intention — 51% accuracy at 3.41 significant strikes per minute — but his output has been wildly inconsistent, ranging from a lively 4.97 strikes per minute against Sharaf to a near-silent 1.70 against Tokkos.
- His knockout power is real but concentrated in a narrow window. Both UFC finishes came in Round 2, and he showed genuine one-punch danger against Parker Porter (R1) and Sean Sharaf. When he's cooking early, opponents are in trouble.
- The grappling picture is stark and damaging: zero takedown attempts in his UFC career, and he has been submitted four times across his last seven fights — twice by Billy Elekana, once by Tuco Tokkos, and once by Valter Walker. He has no answer when this fight hits the canvas.
- His striking defense sits at 51%, which means he absorbs nearly as many clean shots as he lands. He's been stopped by ground-and-pound (de Lima, R2) and knocked around before subs finish him, suggesting he takes damage cleanly before going down.
- At 90 days out, the layoff is a non-issue. The problem is the trend: three losses in his last four, two of which ended on his back in the second round, and a line that has moved dramatically away from him despite the market initially opening him as a heavy favorite.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Kevin Christian -- Style & Profile

- Christian is a 6'7" heavyweight-framed light heavyweight with a five-inch reach advantage over Tafa. At that height and reach, he can make the standing fight uncomfortable — but his career striking output of 1.64 per minute says he rarely commits to extended exchanges on the feet.
- His submission game is his identity. He averages 2.3 submission attempts per 15 minutes and has finished two of his three UFC opponents with submissions. His path to victory in almost every fight runs through the grappling.
- The problem is that he can't get there with any reliability. His UFC takedown accuracy sits at 0%, and both of his last two fights logged zero takedowns landed. He's finishing subs from scrambles and ground transitions, not from controlled wrestling.
- His striking defense is genuinely poor at 44%, and he absorbs 2.65 significant strikes per minute. When he faced Billy Elekana — the same Elekana who just submitted Tafa — Christian was put away in Round 1 by the same submission game. He is not a durable fighter in pure striking exchanges.
- At 181 days since his last fight, there's a modest ring rust consideration. More pressing: his UFC sample is only two fights, which means almost everything we know about him at this level comes from a loss and a win against limited opposition.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central tension in this matchup is that Tafa's best weapon — his hands — is also what gets him into trouble. He can't or won't grapple, and Christian's entire game is built around getting opponents to the ground. Tafa's 70% takedown defense is the one number that actually helps him here; if he can deny Christian entries, he can keep this a punching match where he holds the power and volume advantage. But "70% TD defense" against a fighter who doesn't shoot double-legs and instead looks for scrambles and clinch-based trips is a misleading stat — Tafa's four submission losses suggest the takedown defense number overstates his grappling security.

Christian holds a meaningful reach advantage at 80 inches to Tafa's 75. At 6'7", he can be a difficult puzzle for a shorter striker who needs to close distance to land. Tafa's approach of walking forward and throwing will require him to eat long punches on the way in — and with 44% strike defense on Christian's side, there will be openings — but the question is whether Tafa can avoid getting tangled up when he does close. Christian's striking numbers in the last fight (3.94 per minute, 82.4% accuracy against Elekana) are an outlier that shouldn't be over-weighted from one fight. His career average of 1.64 per minute is who he really is on the feet.

The path to victory for Tafa is a clean early finish — land a big right hand in rounds one or two before the fight transitions to grappling. His knockout wins have all come inside two rounds, and his danger is front-loaded. Christian's path is grinding into the clinch, surviving the power shots, and finding a scramble or body-lock takedown that puts Tafa on his back — which, given the evidence, is not a low-probability plan. The net intangible picture marginally favors Tafa on activity and layoff freshness, but the trend of his last four fights is a significant concern. Both fighters have real vulnerabilities; this is less a "Tafa dominates" setup and more a stylistic coin-flip with Tafa holding the striking edge and Christian holding the grappling edge.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 — Junior Tafa wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: High
Why: Tafa's power is genuine and he's finished opponents quickly when his striking lands clean. Christian's 44% strike defense and history of absorbing damage before submissions mean he can be stopped if Tafa lands a clean combination before the grappling develops. All three of Tafa's wins have come by KO/TKO inside two rounds, and his best scenario is getting the stoppage before Christian can drag this to the mat.

**#2 — Kevin Christian wins by submission, Round 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Tafa has been submitted in four of his seven fights, and two of those came in the second round when his pace had already declined. Christian's submission attempts per minute is one of the higher marks at light heavyweight, and if he can survive the early power shots and get the clinch, a scramble-based finish is very much in play. The parallel with how Elekana finished both of them — via submission in the second round — is worth noting.

**#3 — Kevin Christian wins by submission, Round 1**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Christian finished Elekana's opponent (and was himself finished by Elekana in Round 1), and early sub wins are in his profile. If Tafa is reckless early, dives in without establishing his jab, or gets caught in a scramble after an exchange, Christian can finish inside five minutes. Less likely because Tafa's danger will keep Christian cautious early.

**#4 — Junior Tafa wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Tafa's power doesn't land clean and Christian survives into deep water, Tafa's volume (when active) can accumulate over three rounds. He outstruck opponents in the moments he was landing well, and Christian's 1.64 strikes per minute is not a pace that wins competitive decisions. This requires Tafa to stay off the mat all three rounds — unlikely given the history, but possible.

But "70% TD defense" against a fighter who doesn't shoot double-legs and instead looks for scrambles and clinch-based trips is a misleading stat — Tafa's four submission losses suggest the takedown defense number overstates his grappling security.

Christian holds a meaningful reach advantage at 80 inches to Tafa's 75. At 6'7", he can be a difficult puzzle for a shorter striker who needs to close distance to land. Tafa's approach of walking forward and throwing will require him to eat long punches on the way in — and with 44% strike defense on Christian's side, there will be openings — but the question is whether Tafa can avoid getting tangled up when he does close. Christian's striking numbers in the last fight (3.94 per minute, 82.4% accuracy against Elekana) are an outlier that shouldn't be over-weighted from one fight. His career average of 1.64 per minute is who he really is on the feet.

The path to victory for Tafa is a clean early finish — land a big right hand in rounds one or two before the fight transitions to grappling. His knockout wins have all come inside two rounds, and his danger is front-loaded. Christian's path is grinding into the clinch, surviving the power shots, and finding a scramble or body-lock takedown that puts Tafa on his back — which, given the evidence, is not a low-probability plan. The net intangible picture marginally favors Tafa on activity and layoff freshness, but the trend of his last four fights is a significant concern. Both fighters have real vulnerabilities; this is less a "Tafa dominates" setup and more a stylistic coin-flip with Tafa holding the striking edge and Christian holding the grappling edge.
<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 — Junior Tafa wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: High
Why: Tafa's power is genuine and he's finished opponents quickly when his striking lands clean. Christian's 44% strike defense and history of absorbing damage before submissions mean he can be stopped if Tafa lands a clean combination before the grappling develops. All three of Tafa's wins have come by KO/TKO inside two rounds, and his best scenario is getting the stoppage before Christian can drag this to the mat.

**#2 — Kevin Christian wins by submission, Round 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Tafa has been submitted in four of his seven fights, and two of those came in the second round when his pace had already declined. Christian's submission attempts per minute is one of the higher marks at light heavyweight, and if he can survive the early power shots and get the clinch, a scramble-based finish is very much in play. The parallel with how Elekana finished both of them — via submission in the second round — is worth noting.

**#3 — Kevin Christian wins by submission, Round 1**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Christian finished Elekana's opponent (and was himself finished by Elekana in Round 1), and early sub wins are in his profile. If Tafa is reckless early, dives in without establishing his jab, or gets caught in a scramble after an exchange, Christian can finish inside five minutes. Less likely because Tafa's danger will keep Christian cautious early.

**#4 — Junior Tafa wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Tafa's power doesn't land clean and Christian survives into deep water, Tafa's volume (when active) can accumulate over three rounds. He outstruck opponents in the moments he was landing well, and Christian's 1.64 strikes per minute is not a pace that wins competitive decisions. This requires Tafa to stay off the mat all three rounds — unlikely given the history, but possible.
<!--END-->

**Recommendation: LEAN — Kevin Christian**
Confidence: 54%

The market has moved dramatically since opening. Tafa opened at -400 and has drifted all the way to -225 across most books, while Christian has moved from +205 to +185 on the best lines. That is a significant shift in implied probability — Tafa's open implied roughly 80% confidence; the current line implies about 68-69% after de-vig. That's a large move away from the original favorite, and the sharps have clearly been loading up on Christian or fading Tafa.

**The case for Christian at +185:** Four of Tafa's last seven fights ended in submission, including both of his last two. Christian's submission attempts per minute and his track record of finishing scrambles align almost perfectly with the hole in Tafa's game. Christian's size, reach, and grappling style give him a genuine path. At +185, the implied probability is around 35% — which feels too low given how predictably Tafa ends up on his back.

**The case against the play:** Christian has only two UFC fights. His striking defense is poor. He was finished in Round 1 by the same Billy Elekana who just finished Tafa, and his takedown numbers are zero. His submission wins come from scrambles, not controlled wrestling, which means he needs Tafa to cooperate in some way. Tafa's power in the first two rounds is real and could end this before the grappling develops.

- Assessed probability: ~40%

- Target line: +150 or better (value disappears below that)

**Props:** No prop value identified with the available data. A "fight ends by submission" prop would align with both fighters' grappling histories and Tafa's specific vulnerability, but without de-vigged prop lines available, no recommendation can be made.

**What would change this lean:** If Tafa's camp reports confirmed dedicated anti-grappling work, or if Christian's takedown numbers showed improvement from earlier regional fights, the lean softens toward Tafa. If Christian's reach and size advantage shows up early in the clinch game, the lean strengthens. The submission history on Tafa's side is the load-bearing piece of this read — if that's anomalous rather than structural, the pick is wrong.

## Jacob Malkoun -- Style & Profile

- Malkoun is a volume-first striker who also carries genuine grappling threat — his career average of 5.81 takedowns per 15 minutes is elite for the division, though his 40% accuracy and 33% takedown defense suggest the wrestling is more opportunistic than systematic. His striking output of 4.55 significant strikes per minute at 58% accuracy is the more reliable weapon.
- His last-3 splits show a fighter in transition. Against Finney he was crisp and accurate (62%, 4.07 SLpM) but abandoned the wrestling entirely. Against Petroski he was sluggish (46%, 2.65 SLpM) and won with a single clean power shot in round two. The consistent thread is that he controls fights when active, but his activity level swings significantly fight to fight.
- He walks forward and initiates. Every finish and most of his decision wins come from pressuring opponents to the fence, mixing punches with takedown threats. When he's sharp, opponents can't settle — when he's passive, he coasts on output alone.
- His defensive numbers are concerning at the career level — 33% takedown defense is genuinely poor, and his 55% strike defense is middling — but none of his recent opponents have been credible enough threats in those areas to expose it meaningfully.
- He has been knocked out cold by Phil Hawes in 18 seconds, which is the one real durability flag on his record. One R1 KO loss, one R1 DQ loss — his early-round focus can be inconsistent. That said, he bounced back with three wins, including the KO of Petroski, and he is only 30.
- Active with a 90-day turnaround, no weight issues noted, and fighting at home in Australia. The crowd will be behind him.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Gerald Meerschaert -- Style & Profile

- Meerschaert built his UFC reputation as a submission artist — 1.4 submission attempts per 15 minutes and three submission wins in his recent results against Shahbazyan, Barberena, and Bruno Silva. But look at his last three fights and that identity has almost completely vanished: 0 takedown attempts, near-zero striking output, and three straight losses, two of them finishes.
- His striking numbers over his last three fights are alarming — 0.98 SLpM against Oleksiejczuk, 0.53 against Tavares, and 0.0 against Daukaus before being submitted in 50 seconds. He is not competing offensively anymore. Whether that's injury, age, or motivation, the output has collapsed.
- He has now been knocked out by Oleksiejczuk in round one and submitted by Daukaus in 50 seconds in back-to-back fights. Those are not losses — those are a fighter who is no longer able to impose himself before damage occurs. At 38 years old, with accumulated losses piling up, the physical decline is written in the data.
- His career striking defense of 53% and absorption rate of 3.52 significant strikes per minute were already liabilities when he was winning. Against a volume striker pressing forward, he historically gets hit a lot — and now he appears unable to avoid exchanges or get the fight to the mat.
- His only path to relevance in this fight is a submission finish, and his takedown accuracy sits at 30% career — meaning he needs to get lucky or capitalize on a scramble. Malkoun, despite poor TDD numbers, has not been submitted in his UFC career.
- At 38, with four consecutive losses, two finishes in the last two fights, and a 4-inch reach advantage that will mean nothing if he can't threaten forward — this is a fighter at the very end of the line.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The structural problem for Meerschaert is simple: his primary weapon is the submission, but he has to get the fight to the ground to use it, and Malkoun is the one who actually takes people down. Malkoun averages nearly 6 takedowns per 15 minutes. Meerschaert defends 42% of takedown attempts against him. That math means Malkoun is likely to spend significant time on top if he wants to — and from the top, his ground-and-pound is where his only UFC finish came against Petroski. Meerschaert's submission threat from his back is real in theory, but he hasn't successfully worked off his back against credible opposition recently. More critically, Meerschaert hasn't attempted a single takedown in his last three fights — whatever wrestling game he once deployed has disappeared entirely.

On the feet, Malkoun has every advantage that matters in 2026. He's eight years younger, he throws significantly more volume (4.55 vs. 3.01 SLpM career), he's more accurate (58% vs. 45%), and he absorbs less (2.36 vs. 3.52 SLpM). Meerschaert has a 4-inch reach advantage but has shown no ability to use reach as a defensive tool lately — he absorbed a round-one KO from Oleksiejczuk and was strangled in 50 seconds by Daukaus. A reach advantage only matters if you're using it to control range, and Meerschaert's recent fights suggest he is no longer doing that. Malkoun walking forward against a fighter who has been getting stopped early is a straightforward winning formula.

Meerschaert's path to winning requires Malkoun to either load up sloppily and fall into a guillotine or rear-naked choke off a scramble — a real enough scenario to price at greater than zero, but not one supported by Malkoun's fight history. Malkoun's path is simply to do what he does: press forward, land volume, and either grind out rounds or find a finish. The intangible delta strongly favors Malkoun — 8 years younger, active, at home, on a two-fight winning streak. Meerschaert carries every negative momentum indicator in the book.

Likelihood: High
Why: Malkoun's most common winning pattern is controlling the pace for three full rounds rather than forcing a finish. Against a fighter who is no longer threatening offensively, the likely outcome is that Malkoun lands volume strikes, walls off any scrambles, and coasts to a comfortable decision. His last three fights — including the Finney decision — follow this pattern.

Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Meerschaert has been finished in round one in two of his last three fights, and his defensive output has evaporated. The market's heavy lean suggests scouts and sharp money expect a short night.

**#3 — Gerald Meerschaert wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: This is Meerschaert's only realistic path — a desperate scramble or a sloppy Malkoun takedown attempt that turns into a choke. His submission credentials are legitimate at career level (three UFC submissions), and Malkoun's 33% takedown defense is a real vulnerability. But Meerschaert hasn't attempted a takedown in three fights and has shown no ability to get this fight to the mat against a physically superior opponent pressing forward.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Colby Thicknesse -- Style & Profile

- At 26, Thicknesse is early in his UFC run with only two fights on record — one encouraging, one ugly. The contrast is stark: against Musasa he was accurate and active (57% striking accuracy, 2.87 strikes/min), but against Topuria he essentially disappeared (0.87 strikes/min, 28% accuracy). He has not yet shown consistency at this level.
- His grappling numbers are thin and largely unreliable. A 9% takedown accuracy across his UFC fights tells you he's attempting them without converting — either the level change is telegraphed or the technique isn't there yet against UFC-caliber opposition.
- He is a forward-moving fighter who likes to initiate, but his output against pressure drops significantly. Topuria pushed the pace and Thicknesse had no answer — his offense cratered when he couldn't dictate terms.
- At 26 with an 8-1 record and two UFC fights, the ceiling is genuinely unknown. He can look sharp (the Musasa fight had clean moments), but the floor — as shown against Topuria — is low enough to give pause against a veteran with finishing power.
- The 216-day layoff is moderate. Nothing alarming, but he hasn't fought frequently enough to call him sharp or ring-rusty with confidence.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Vince Morales -- Style & Profile

- Morales has lost five straight fights, all by unanimous decision, and the last three splits are alarming: in those bouts he's averaging 0.67 significant strikes per minute combined — essentially invisible offensively. Whatever striking game he once had, it is not showing up in competition right now.
- His career striking absorption rate (4.00 strikes absorbed per minute) is significantly higher than what he lands (3.57/min), meaning he's consistently on the wrong side of the exchange volume math. He does not have the defense to survive as the busier striker in a fight.
- At 35, he's at the age where bantamweights typically begin their decline — reflexes, chin durability, and recovery all erode simultaneously. Five decision losses in a row, with virtually no offensive output in his last three, suggests the decline is already underway.
- His only UFC knockout win came against Louis Smolka in Round 1 — a fight with a live-wire finisher on both sides. His submission average is negligible (0.5 attempts per 15 minutes, zero finishes in recent history), and his takedown numbers are sparse. There is no reliable finishing mechanism to point to right now.
- He's coming off a 398-day layoff — well over a year out. Combined with the age factor and the losing streak, there is meaningful ring rust risk here regardless of what caused the absence.
- He was knocked out by Chris Gutierrez in Round 2, and while that's a single data point, the combination of accumulated decision losses, age, and poor defensive numbers (53% strike defense, absorbing 4.00/min) raises legitimate concern about his durability against anyone with pop.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The structural read here is fairly clear: Thicknesse is younger, more active when allowed to operate on his terms, and is facing a fighter in measurable decline. Morales has not generated meaningful offensive output in three consecutive fights — his last three per-fight SLpM figures of 0.0, 1.27, and 0.73 are not the numbers of a fighter finding his footing, they're the numbers of a fighter being completely shut down. Thicknesse, even in his worst UFC outing (the Topuria loss), was generating more than that. The volume gap, adjusted for current form rather than career averages, favors Thicknesse meaningfully.

The concern for Thicknesse is whether he can impose his pace consistently. Against Topuria, he was neutralized — his output fell off a cliff. If Morales, despite the losing streak, can match Thicknesse's pace early and make this uncomfortable, there's a scenario where Thicknesse again retreats into passivity. That said, Morales's recent opponents (Rosas Jr., Elijah Smith, Lapilus, Miles Johns, Jonathan Martinez) are largely better than Thicknesse's opposition, and Morales still couldn't generate anything against them. The talent gap against those opponents may explain some of the output, but the volume numbers are still damning.

The age delta is real — nine years separates these two, and Morales is on the wrong side of 35 at bantamweight. A 398-day layoff compounds the rust concern substantially. If the fight plays out at a moderate pace over three rounds, Thicknesse should have the fresher legs, the more current momentum (he's on a win in his last outing), and the physical advantage. Morales's most realistic path is landing something significant early, but his career KO rate against any fighter with reasonable defense has been minimal, and Thicknesse at 26 with no known chin issues is not an obvious knockout target. The intangible delta here — youth vs. age, activity vs. layoff — leans toward Thicknesse.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Colby Thicknesse wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: Both fighters are predominantly decision-setters, and Morales's last five fights have all gone the distance with him on the losing end. Thicknesse should be the busier, fresher fighter across three rounds given the age and layoff gaps. This fight looks likely to be a grind that Thicknesse controls on volume without ever finding a clean finish.

**#2 -- Colby Thicknesse wins by TKO/KO**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Morales absorbs 4.00 significant strikes per minute over his UFC career and has been stopped by TKO before (Gutierrez in R2). If Thicknesse can operate at his Musasa-level output rather than his Topuria-level output, he may accumulate enough punishment to force a late stoppage. Not a primary path, but Morales's defensive numbers leave it open.

**#3 -- Vince Morales wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Low

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Ben Johnston -- Style & Profile

- Johnston is making his UFC debut at 35 years old, which means there is no UFC statistical footprint to analyze — every number in his profile reads zero because he simply hasn't fought in the Octagon yet. All reads on him come from his 5-1 pre-UFC record.
- His pre-UFC record shows five wins but the regional competition level is unknown, which limits how much weight those wins can carry. A 5-1 record in regional Australian/international MMA is a different credential than 5-1 in Bellator or DWCS — treat it accordingly.
- At 35, he is entering the UFC at an age when most middleweights are already on the back nine of their careers. The combination of debut adjustment shock and age-related athletic decline compounds the challenge of stepping into a cage with an already-experienced UFC fighter.
- He holds a 6'4" frame at 185 lbs, which is unusually long for middleweight. If his pre-UFC style leaned on that length — keeping range with the jab, throwing from distance — it could translate. But we have no UFC tape to confirm whether he actually uses his physical tools the way his build suggests he might.
- Without verified finish data from his pre-UFC fights in this dataset, we cannot establish whether he is a finisher or a decision fighter. That's a genuine analytical blind spot here — his preferred path to victory is unknown.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Wes Schultz -- Style & Profile

- Schultz's last three fights tell a volatile, difficult story: he was knocked out in Round 2 by Abdul-Malik, then submitted Mingaj in under two minutes, and most recently was stopped in Round 1 inside 2:30 by Damian Pinas. Two KO/TKO losses in his last three fights, both coming in the first two rounds, is a serious red flag on his chin.
- The strike absorption numbers back up the concern. He absorbs 4.97 significant strikes per minute for his UFC career while landing 4.60 — he's essentially in a trade-off brawl every time he fights, and he's getting slightly the worse of the exchanges. His 45% strike defense is poor for a middleweight who keeps ending up in firefights.
- His last fight against Pinas — a 1-2 UFC fighter at the time — saw him land only 23% of his strikes and eat a finish in 2:30 of Round 1. Getting knocked out by someone with modest KO credentials is exactly the pattern that signals a chin that has genuinely eroded.
- The one clear bright spot is his grappling when he commits to it. Against Mingaj, he attempted a takedown at an extraordinary rate (16.22 per 15 minutes) and finished with a submission in less than two minutes. The problem is that in two of his last three fights he never attempted a single takedown, which suggests the wrestling is situational rather than a reliable go-to when he's under pressure.
- At 4.52 takedowns per 15 minutes career average but only 25% accuracy, he shoots often but misses most of them — which is an energy expenditure problem in a sport where failed takedown attempts burn gas fast.
- He is 29, which removes the age-decay concern, but the accumulated KO damage is the overriding issue. He has been finished by strikes twice in three fights, and one of those was an early Round 1 stoppage — the pattern is consistent enough to take seriously.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central problem with this matchup is the information asymmetry. Ben Johnston has zero UFC data — no strikes, no takedowns, no finishes — which makes any structural read on how these styles actually collide quite thin. What we do know is that Schultz is an established UFC fighter with a defined pattern of behavior, and Johnston is an unknown quantity stepping in at 35. In any debut matchup, the experienced fighter carries an adjustment advantage, but that edge is complicated here because Schultz himself is coming off a damaging knockout loss just 62 days ago.

The clearest read on Schultz is that he becomes dangerous the moment he can get the fight to the ground. His submission of Mingaj demonstrated an ability to move at high speed from takedown to finish on the mat. The question is whether he can execute that against a 6'4" opponent whose long frame creates real difficulties for single-leg entries and whose reach off his back might complicate ground-and-pound setups. If Johnston has any defensive wrestling, Schultz defaults to a striking exchange — and that is the worst version of this fight for Schultz, given what has happened to him in striking battles recently. The intangible that hangs over everything is that Schultz fought and was knocked out 62 days ago. That is an extremely short turnaround from a TKO loss, and the potential for residual effects — physical or psychological — is real.

Johnston's path to winning this fight runs through either using his 6'4" frame to keep range and land meaningful strikes before Schultz closes distance, or wrestling Schultz himself if his pre-UFC record includes any grappling. The honest answer is we don't know enough about Johnston to map his path in detail — the lack of UFC data forces us to bet on what we know rather than what we can project. What we know is that Schultz has been finished twice in three fights, is coming back on very short rest from a KO loss, and tends to absorb punishment at a high rate. Johnston's unknown quantities create real uncertainty, but Schultz is entering with observable vulnerabilities.

This is the broadest outcome bucket because we simply don't know Johnston's finishing style, but the structural setup favors him if he stays upright.

Why: Schultz's best and most decisive UFC performance was a 1:51 Round 1 submission, and when he gets desperate or senses opportunity on the feet, he has shown an ability to close the distance fast and go for the takedown aggressively. If Johnston is inexperienced on the ground — a real possibility given the lack of visible grappling credentials in this dataset — Schultz can exploit the debut nerves and end it quickly.

**#3 — Wes Schultz wins by KO/TKO, Round 1–2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Schultz does land 4.60 significant strikes per minute and has some offensive output in exchanges. If Johnston's debut nerves manifest early and he walks into shots, Schultz can finish fights — though his recent history suggests he's far more likely to get stopped than to stop others in striking battles.

**#4 — Ben Johnston wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Johnston has the range management to use his 6'4" frame and keep Schultz at the end of punches for three rounds without a finish, he could win on the cards. Schultz's 45% strike defense gives a reaching opponent real opportunities to land, and Johnston might simply outwork him over three rounds if neither fighter commits to a finishing sequence.

## Jonathan Micallef -- Style & Profile

- Micallef is a grappler at heart, but not in the way his career averages suggest. His last three fights show striking volumes of 1.29, 1.67, and 0.33 per minute — he's not interested in trading on the feet. He hunts the clinch and the takedown, and once he gets there, he's dangerous. Two of his last three wins ended on the mat.
- His submission average of 1.1 per 15 minutes is the most reliable number in his profile. He's a finisher from top control — the Oban Elliott and Mohamed Ado wins both came via submission, and Ado was put away in three minutes. He doesn't need rounds to get the job done once the fight hits the floor.
- His striking accuracy of 41% career-wide drops significantly in recent fights (25–30% in two of his last three), which fits the profile of someone who strikes to set up takedowns rather than to damage. He's not a threat on the feet in any meaningful sense — his striking is a means to an end.
- As a southpaw, his left hand and left kick have a structural angle advantage over an orthodox opponent, but his numbers don't suggest he exploits that aggressively. The stance is more of a quiet asymmetry than an active weapon.
- At 27, he's ascending. Three straight UFC wins, all finishes or clear decisions. He hasn't been tested by anyone with world-class defensive grappling, but he hasn't needed to be — none of his opponents have been able to keep the fight standing long enough to find out.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Themba Gorimbo -- Style & Profile

- Gorimbo's career UFC striking averages look elite on paper — 60% accuracy, only 1.32 absorbed per minute — but those numbers are built on a foundation that his last two fights have quietly demolished. Against Luque, he landed 5.77 strikes per minute but was submitted in 52 seconds. Against Wells, he managed 0.27 per minute on 100% accuracy — which means he attempted almost nothing and got outworked for 15 minutes.
- His takedown numbers tell a genuinely strange story. Career average of 5.07 takedowns per 15 minutes is outstanding, but his last three fights show TD rates of 4.0, 0.0, and 1.0. He wrestled against Wells and lost a decision anyway. Against Luque, he abandoned the takedown entirely and paid for it with a first-round submission.
- At 35, he's at or near the age where welterweights start losing a step — not in obvious ways at first, but in the margins that decide fights. Back-to-back losses heading into this one, both in ways that expose different problems: can't win a wrestling decision, can't survive on the feet against a sub threat.
- The Luque loss is the most alarming recent data point. Submitted in 52 seconds in Round 1 is not a cardio problem — it's a submission defense problem in live exchanges. Against a fighter who generates 1.1 submission attempts per 15 minutes, that matters enormously.
- His 72% takedown defense is competent but not elite. If Micallef gets to his preferred range and starts mixing in clinch entries, Gorimbo's path to controlling the grappling exchanges gets significantly harder.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The core question in this fight is whether Gorimbo can keep it standing long enough to win a decision, because the moment the fight hits the mat, the dynamic shifts heavily in Micallef's favor. Gorimbo's most dangerous version is a wrestling-heavy pressure fighter who controls opponents against the fence — but that's not what he's looked like lately. Against Wells he tried it and lost. Against Luque he abandoned it entirely and got submitted before the first round ended. Micallef, meanwhile, has finished two of his last three opponents by submission, and his ground game is the clearest skill advantage either fighter holds in this matchup.

Gorimbo's best path is the striking game, where his accuracy and output numbers are genuinely better than Micallef's. Micallef doesn't defend strikes particularly well — 56% career defense — and absorbs 4.22 per minute, which is a meaningful exposure. If Gorimbo can stay disciplined, keep the fight in the pocket, and avoid the fence clinch that bleeds into takedowns, he can accumulate damage and win rounds on the feet. But his own strike defense sits at 51%, and Micallef's southpaw angle creates awkward looks that orthodox fighters tend to underestimate until they've already been hit.

The age gap is real and worth factoring in. Micallef is 27 and improving; Gorimbo is 35 and coming off consecutive losses. The intangibles stack modestly in Micallef's direction — he's the younger, ascending fighter, in front of a home crowd in Perth that will be loud for him. Gorimbo's submission defense has been exposed twice in his last seven fights (Luque, AJ Fletcher), and against a fighter who actively hunts submissions from top position, that's a structural problem he can't paper over with athletic effort. The fight shapes up as Gorimbo needing a 15-minute striking masterclass to win, versus Micallef needing one clean takedown sequence. Based on how both men have fought recently, the latter is more realistic.

Likelihood: High
Why: Micallef's pattern is consistent — he keeps striking volume low, gets the fight to the ground, and finishes with submissions inside two rounds. If Micallef gets one clean takedown or clinch entry, the finishing sequence is well within his capabilities.

Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Gorimbo's wrestling holds and neither man can decisively impose their grappling, this becomes a grinding, low-volume affair. Micallef's southpaw angles and Gorimbo's diminished recent output (0.27 strikes per minute against Wells) suggest a fight that stays close and ugly but scores in Micallef's favor over three rounds.

**#3 -- Gorimbo wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Gorimbo's path requires staying disciplined on the feet, avoiding every clinch and fence entry, and out-landing Micallef over three rounds. His 60% career accuracy is genuinely dangerous if he commits to striking — Micallef absorbs over four strikes per minute. But Gorimbo's recent inactivity on the feet against Wells suggests he can't sustain that output for three full rounds anymore.

**#4 -- Gorimbo wins by KO/TKO**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Micallef does absorb strikes at a relatively high rate, and Gorimbo has one first-round KO on his resume (Pete Rodriguez). analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Kody Steele -- Style & Profile

- Steele's UFC career averages tell a concerning story on defense: he absorbs 7.17 significant strikes per minute — one of the higher absorption rates you'll see at lightweight — while his strike defense sits at just 52%. He gets hit, a lot, and often.
- His striking accuracy is only 32% on 4.44 attempts per minute, which means he's throwing volume but not landing efficiently. His last two UFC fights confirm the trend: 31.4% and 33.9% accuracy in those outings. He's busy but not precise.
- His one highlight in the UFC was a Round 2 KO of Chasen Blair in October 2024, which is real — but his most recent fight was a unanimous decision loss to Rongzhu in February 2025, where his output dropped all the way to 1.8 strikes per minute. That's a fighter who got completely controlled and outworked.
- His grappling is almost a non-factor offensively: 0.62 takedowns per 15 minutes on 14% accuracy. He doesn't take people down and when he does attempt it, he rarely succeeds. His 100% takedown defense is the one genuinely strong number on his sheet, but that's largely irrelevant if his opponent doesn't shoot.
- He's coming off 447 days out of action — over a full calendar year on the shelf. That kind of layoff introduces real rust risk, particularly in timing and rhythm, which matters most for a fighter whose game depends on volume output that's already inconsistent.
- At 31, he's not at an age cliff, but the layoff compounded with a loss and declining output in his last outing makes him a fighter trending in the wrong direction heading into this fight.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central problem with analyzing this fight is that one half of it is almost entirely opaque. Dom Mar Fan is making his UFC debut with no parsed fight history, no UFC statistics, and no confirmed style identity. What we do know is that he has a four-inch reach advantage — the exact threshold where range starts to matter structurally — and he comes in off a clean weight cut against an opponent who has real, documented vulnerabilities. That's not nothing, even when the data is thin.

Steele's numbers are the readable side of this matchup, and they paint a picture of a fighter under pressure. He absorbs more than seven significant strikes per minute, which is alarming regardless of context. His strike defense at 52% means roughly half of what opponents throw at him is landing clean. In his loss to Rongzhu, he put up just 1.8 strikes per minute — a complete offensive shutdown. He's been knocked out before (Blair beat him, and Blair also lost to Rongzhu), and the combination of poor defense, declining output, and over a year away from competition makes him genuinely vulnerable. If Street Buddha is any kind of active striker with range management, Steele's defensive numbers give him a real path to a finish.

The honest caveat is that we can't confirm Street Buddha has the tools to exploit those vulnerabilities. We're betting on unknown upside against known downside. Steele's layoff, absorption rate, and last-fight performance point toward a fighter who's beatable. Street Buddha's reach, clean weight cut, and unbeaten-ish regional record give him credibility. The line has moved heavily toward Steele despite no public MMA data confirming he's significantly improved — that movement likely reflects UFC experience familiarity rather than sharp money identifying real edge. The intangible delta here slightly favors Street Buddha on layoff concerns alone (Steele, 447 days out) versus a debuting fighter who at least has been active.

- He's coming off 447 days out of action — over a full calendar year on the shelf. That kind of layoff introduces real rust risk, particularly in timing and rhythm, which matters most for a fighter whose game depends on volume output that's already inconsistent.
- At 31, he's not at an age cliff, but the layoff compounded with a loss and declining output in his last outing makes him a fighter trending in the wrong direction heading into this fight.
